statement.markdown: Implement a method that returns the greatest common divisor of two integers using the Euclidean algorithm. The function should correctly handle all cases, including those where the inputs are close to the maximum or minimum integer value. Note that the function uses the absolute value of the input integers.
statement.notes: The Euclidean algorithm is a well-known method for computing the greatest common divisor of two integers, and should be used here. It iteratively replaces `n` with `m` and `m` with the remainder of `n` divided by `m` (with the sign of the remainder being the same as `m`), until `m` is 0. At that point, `n` is the GCD.

hints.text: The function will not handle the edge case where n is m. This should be considered as a valid input.

editorial.title: Euclidean Algorithm
editorial.intuition: Use the absolute value of the inputs to ensure the Euclidean algorithm works correctly for all cases, including negative numbers.
editorial.bruteForce: Iteratively apply the Euclidean algorithm until m is 0.
editorial.betterSolution: Use the built-in math.gcd function in Python.
editorial.optimalSolution: Directly return the absolute value of the smaller number when the absolute value of the remainder is less than the smaller number.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The Euclidean algorithm is mathematically sound and will always produce the correct GCD.
editorial.dryRun: Run the function with the example input (12, 15).
commonMistakes: Not handling the edge case where n is m.
Not using the absolute value of the inputs.

functionSignature.python: def gcd(self, n: int, m: int) -> int
